bullets: Data centers house compute, networking, storage and supporting systems required to run digital services.
AI workloads (training and inference) increase compute density and continuous power demand compared with typical IT loads.
Higher density leads to greater cooling and power distribution requirements inside facilities.
Growth in AI usage is a major driver of new hyperscale and modular data center builds.
Operators balance latency, reliability and cost when siting and designing AI-capable facilities.
takeaways: Expect rising peak and sustained power demand from AI-driven data center growth—plan grid capacity upgrades and interconnection accordingly.
Cooling and power distribution design will increasingly determine feasibility and cost of AI-capable sites; EPCs should prioritize high-density systems.
Renewable integration, on-site generation and flexible procurement will be key to meeting energy needs while managing carbon and cost.
Utilities and planners should engage earlier with hyperscalers and cloud providers to coordinate transmission, substations and resilience measures.
